nativeRange.habitat: Reclusive reef dweller on offshore coral and rocky reefs at depths of roughly 1–25 m; tends to remain close to rockwork and is quick to retreat into crevices when startled.

diet.notes: Generally considered an unfussy micropredator that has been reported to readily accept most prepared aquarium foods, including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, finely chopped meaty seafood, and quality carnivore-formulated marine pellet or flake preparations. Has been widely documented preying on bristleworms in the display, which is often regarded as a useful side effect, but the same predatory behavior extends to small ornamental shrimp. Generally advised to feed 1–2 small varied meals per day.

summary: A vibrant electric-blue-and-orange dottyback from the western Indian Ocean and Persian Gulf, generally considered hardy and beginner-friendly but notably semi-aggressive and territorial. Best kept singly in a tank with ample rockwork.
careNotes:
The Neon Dottyback (also widely sold as the Orange Dottyback) is generally considered one of the hardier, more beginner-accessible saltwater fish — it adapts readily to captive foods, tolerates the standard reef parameter window, and is reliably available in the trade as both wild-caught and captive-bred specimens. LiveAquaria explicitly notes that the species is now "very seldom harvested from the wild" and is most commonly offered captive-bred. Multiple sources note that captive-bred dottybacks tend to be noticeably less aggressive than wild-caught individuals.

Despite its small size, the species has been widely reported as semi-aggressive and territorial: it will defend its rockwork against similar-shaped tankmates and slower fish, and conspecific aggression is severe. Keep one per tank, and do not combine with other dottybacks. It is generally advised to be added last to a community tank. Reef compatibility is generally considered good with corals and most sessile invertebrates, but the species has been reliably reported to prey on small ornamental shrimp; larger cleaner / fire shrimp tend to coexist safely.

A tightly sealed lid is generally advised — multiple care guides note Neon Dottybacks have been reported to jump from open tanks when spooked or chased. Plenty of live rock with numerous caves, crevices, and overhangs is required to give it a defendable home territory.

Cross-source spread: max size — LiveAquaria reports 4 in (~10 cm), FishBase 10.0 cm TL, BiomesInABox ~4 in; converged on 8–10 cm. Temperature — all three retail/hobby sources state 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase wild range 24–28°C; averaged to 22–27°C. Salinity — 1.020–1.025 SG (universal). Minimum tank — LiveAquaria and BiomesInABox both state 30 US gal (~114 L); other hobby sources recommend 55 gal as ideal given territorial behavior; biased upward to 150 L per the tank-size special rule to provide a defendable territory plus space for tankmates outside its claimed zone. Lifespan is not stated by the primary retail or FishBase entries — the 5–8 year range used here is a conservative interpolation from general Pseudochromidae longevity rather than a directly sourced value.

Nomenclature note: the LiveAquaria page lists family as 'Labridae', which is incorrect — FishBase confirms family Pseudochromidae, order Ovalentaria/misc. FishBase also confirms this species is a bi-directional sex changer, an unusual trait among aquarium fish.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to be bred in captivity by commercial aquaculture facilities (Sea & Reef Aquaculture and ORA among others), and captive-bred specimens now dominate the trade. As with congeners, the male is generally expected to prepare a cave or crevice and court a female who deposits an egg ball inside; the male typically guards the clutch. Home-aquarium breeding is essentially incompatible with a community setup because conspecifics cannot be safely combined in normal-sized tanks. Raising fry is considered moderately challenging due to the small larval size. FishBase additionally documents bi-directional sex change in this species, which may simplify pair formation in dedicated breeding setups.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for confirmed scientific name (Pseudochromis aldabraensis), care level (Easy/beginner), temperament (Semi-Aggressive), reef-safe (Yes), diet (carnivore — varied carnivore-formulated foods, bristleworm predator), max size (4 in / ~10 cm), minimum tank (30 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), captive-bred status (very seldom wild-collected), warning that it 'may eat ornamental shrimp' and should be housed with moderately aggressive larger fish. Note: the LiveAquaria page lists family as 'Labridae' which is incorrect for Pseudochromidae; corrected from FishBase. Reached via the /collections/dottybacks index.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Pseudochromidae, order Ovalentaria/misc), authority (Bauchot-Boutin, 1958), accepted common name 'Orange dottyback', max length 10.0 cm TL, depth 1–25 m, native distribution (Aldabra, Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, Pakistan, Sri Lanka), wild temperature range 24–28°C, reclusive reef habit, IUCN Least Concern (2019), and the notable trait that bi-directional sex change has been confirmed for this species.

additional.name: BiomesInABox (Neon Dottyback complete care guide)
additional.notes: Third confirming source for water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), tank minimum (30 gal), max size (~4 in), reef-safety with the small-shrimp caveat, and the jumping behavior that justifies the escapeRisk: high rating and well-fitted lid recommendation. Confirms severe conspecific aggression (cannot be kept with other dottybacks) and lists compatible tankmates (clownfish, dwarf angelfish, tangs, foxface, gobies).

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawning species; male prepares a hiding site and courts the female, who deposits an egg ball inside the cave that the male typically guards. Has been bred commercially by aquaculture facilities (Sea & Reef, ORA), and captive-bred specimens dominate the trade. Bi-directional sex change has been confirmed for this species (FishBase), which may aid pair formation in dedicated breeding setups. Home-aquarium breeding is impractical in normal community-sized tanks because conspecifics cannot be safely combined.
